ECB SAFE Survey — Vlna 38

Slovensko · Eurozóna · Nemecko  |  Vygenerované 01 Jul 2026

Survey questions collected on a 3-month basis (14 questions)

Július Koller — Pre každú príležitosť... osviežujúci národný podnik. (UFO) (1978)

Zhrnutie

  • Financing cost surge: Non-interest bank charges tightened for a net 41% of Slovak firms, pushing total borrowing costs higher and outpacing the euro area average.
  • Margin squeeze deepens: A net 84% of firms reported rising labour costs while turnover fell for a net 26%, compressing profits for a net 40% — the sharpest cost-revenue divergence in recent waves.
  • Credit line stress widens: The financing gap for credit lines hit 14.8 pp, exceeding the euro area’s 12.6 pp and leaving Slovak firms more liquidity-constrained than peers.
  • Pessimism on future funding: A net 5% of firms expect bank loan availability to deteriorate ahead, with trade credit expectations worsening for a net 10% — both outpacing euro area peers.

Obsah

  • Podmienky financovania
    • Slovak firms report sharp reversal in interest rate conditions, joining broader tightening trend
    • Slovak credit line stress surpassed euro area as financing need jumped sharply
    • Slovak firms expect bank loan availability to deteriorate, outpacing EA pessimism
    • Slovak firms financed working capital and new products far above euro area peers
    • Bank willingness to lend remains positive for Slovak firms but eases
  • Ekonomická situácia firiem
    • Slovak firms report deepening profit squeeze as turnover falls and costs surge
    • Slovak firms' turnover optimism fell sharply while investment expectations held firm
    • Slovak firms expect input prices to outpace selling prices, squeezing margins further
    • Slovak firms see stronger upside inflation risks than euro area peers
    • Slovak firms rate access to finance more pressing while all other concerns ease

Podmienky financovania

Slovak firms report sharp reversal in interest rate conditions, joining broader tightening trend

Changes in Terms and Conditions of Bank Financing (Q10)

  • A net 5% of Slovak firms (n=62) reported tighter interest rates — on bank loans, overdrafts and credit lines — reversing from a net 8% reporting easing in the previous quarter (Δ +13.8 pp), though remaining well below the EA's net 27% and DE's net 38%, putting upward pressure on financing costs for borrowers.
  • Non-interest financing costs (charges, fees, commissions) tightened for a net 41% of Slovak firms (n=60), easing marginally by 1.8 pp from the prior quarter's net 42%, and running above the EA average of net 37%, further increasing the total cost of borrowing.
  • Available loan size tightened for a net 6% of Slovak firms (n=62), easing slightly by 0.9 pp compared with the previous quarter's net 7%, and broadly in line with the EA's net 9%, limiting firms' access to necessary liquidity.

* Túto otázku dostávajú iba firmy, ktoré v minulosti využili alebo žiadali o daný typ financovania. Nižší počet respondentov oproti celkovej vzorke je zámerný a neindikuje problém s kvalitou dát — pozri metodológiu ECB SAFE.

Slovak credit line stress surpassed euro area as financing need jumped sharply

Financing Need vs Availability Gap (Q5/Q9)

  • A net 12.3% of Slovak firms (n=278) reported increased credit line needs — up 9.2 pp from the previous quarter's net 3.1% — widening the financing gap to 14.8 pp and leaving Slovakia more stressed than the EA's 12.6 pp gap, suggesting tighter liquidity constraints for firms.
  • While credit line conditions tightened, the bank loan gap remained negative at -4.0 pp (n=254), meaning availability still outpaced need; Slovakia stayed less stressed than the EA (+1.7 pp gap), though the SK gap did narrow 3.6 pp from the prior quarter's -7.6 pp as need edged less negative — indicating some relief in traditional loan financing.
  • Trade credit stress eased, with the Slovak financing gap falling to +6.3 pp (n=150) from +9.8 pp the prior quarter, driven by a 3.0 pp drop in net need to +7.1% — shifting Slovakia back to a less stressed position relative to the EA's +8.2 pp gap, which may support smoother supply chain operations.

* Túto otázku dostávajú iba firmy, ktoré v minulosti využili alebo žiadali o daný typ financovania. Nižší počet respondentov oproti celkovej vzorke je zámerný a neindikuje problém s kvalitou dát — pozri metodológiu ECB SAFE.

Slovak firms expect bank loan availability to deteriorate, outpacing EA pessimism

Expected Availability of External Financing (Q23)

  • A net 5% of Slovak firms (n=130) expected bank loan availability to deteriorate over the coming period, worsening 3.2 pp from a net 2% in the previous quarter, compared with a net 4% across the EA (n=2,948) — suggesting tighter financing conditions ahead for Slovak borrowers.
  • Slovak firms' pessimism on bank loans now exceeds the EA average, signalling persistent local supply tightness: while both regions moved in the same direction, Slovakia's deterioration expectation is 1.4 pp deeper than the EA's net 4% — putting further pressure on firms reliant on bank financing.
  • Trade credit availability expectations remained sharply negative for Slovak firms, with a net 10% (n=79) anticipating deterioration — roughly twelve times more pessimistic than the EA net 1% (n=1,912), though marginally improved by 1.5 pp from the prior quarter, which could strain supplier relationships and cash flow.

* Túto otázku dostávajú iba firmy, ktoré v minulosti využili alebo žiadali o daný typ financovania. Nižší počet respondentov oproti celkovej vzorke je zámerný a neindikuje problém s kvalitou dát — pozri metodológiu ECB SAFE.

† Bunky s menej ako 10 platnými odpoveďami v danej kombinácii vlny × krajiny × položky sú vynechané z grafu; medzery v sérii indikujú nedostatok dát pre dané obdobie.

Slovak firms financed working capital and new products far above euro area peers

Purpose of Financing (Q6A)

  • 55.7% of Slovak firms (n=229) used financing for inventory and working capital — 16.2 pp above the EA's 39.5% (n=5,256) and broadly stable, easing just 0.1 pp from the previous quarter, suggesting persistent liquidity needs.
  • 32.8% of Slovak firms (n=227) cited developing and launching new products or services, rising 2.9 pp from the previous quarter's 29.9%, compared with only 20.2% (n=5,254) across the euro area, indicating stronger innovation-driven growth ambitions.
  • The share of Slovak firms financing fixed investment fell from 40.3% to 34.2% (n=229), a 6.1 pp decline that brought Slovakia below the EA average of 36.8% for the first time in the two observed waves — highlighting reduced capital expenditure relative to peers.

🤖 Táto sekcia obsahuje dáta získané AI agentom priamym dopytovaním databázy SAFE počas generovania správy.

Bank willingness to lend remains positive for Slovak firms but eases

Factors Affecting Access to External Financing (Q11)

  • A net 7.8% of Slovak firms (n=306) reported that banks' willingness to provide credit improved, easing 2.6 pp from a net 10.4% in the previous quarter — still above the EA's net 5.5% and well above Germany's net 4.4% deterioration, suggesting continued but moderating support for financing access.
  • A net 44.4% of Slovak firms (n=442) reported a deterioration in the general economic outlook, worsening 6.1 pp compared with a net 38.3% in the previous quarter and significantly exceeding the EA's net 26.1% deterioration — a notable macro-level drag on credit supply, putting pressure on firms' ability to secure external financing.
  • Access to public financial support deteriorated for a net 33.8% of Slovak firms (n=298), widening sharply by 8.8 pp from a net 25.0% in the previous quarter, more than double the EA's net 15.5% deterioration and signalling a significant tightening of public guarantee channels.

Ekonomická situácia firiem

Slovak firms report deepening profit squeeze as turnover falls and costs surge

Business Situation Indicators (Q2)

  • A net 26% of Slovak firms (n=229) reported falling turnover, worsening by 11.6 pp from a net 15% in the previous quarter, far below the EA's near-flat net 1% improvement — signalling weaker revenue streams and tighter liquidity.
  • A net 84% of Slovak firms (n=229) reported rising labour costs — up 21.8 pp from a net 62% in the prior quarter — well above the EA's already elevated net 63%, compressing margins further.
  • A net 40% of Slovak firms (n=229) reported declining profits, deteriorating 17.4 pp from a net 22% in the previous quarter, more than double the EA's net 17% profit decline — pointing to unsustainable cost pressures amid shrinking revenue.

Slovak firms' turnover optimism fell sharply while investment expectations held firm

Expected Changes in Turnover and Investment (Q26)

  • A net 7% of Slovak firms (n=228) expected turnover to increase over the coming period, falling 14 pp from a net 21% in the previous wave — diverging markedly from the EA's net 26% (n=5,266), which suggests potential revenue pressures for Slovak firms.
  • Slovak turnover expectations now stand nearly 19 pp below the euro area level, signalling a diverging growth trajectory as EA firms grew more optimistic while Slovak firms turned cautious.
  • By contrast, a net 15% of Slovak firms (n=228) anticipated higher fixed investment, rising 4 pp from a net 11% in the previous wave and outpacing the EA's net 9% — suggesting capital spending plans remain resilient despite weakening revenue expectations.

Slovak firms expect input prices to outpace selling prices, squeezing margins further

Price, Wage and Employment Expectations (Q31/Q34)

  • Slovak firms (n=418) expected average selling prices to rise 4.27% over the next 12 months, easing 0.36 pp from the previous wave's 4.63% — yet still outpacing the EA average of 3.63%, suggesting continued pressure on competitiveness.
  • Slovak firms anticipate 8.31% production input price growth (n=417) alongside 4.21% wage growth (n=441), compressing margins by roughly 8 pp relative to selling price expectations — consistent with a net 84% of firms already reporting rising labour costs in wave 38.
  • Slovak wage growth expectations eased notably, falling from 5.49% in the prior wave to 4.21% (Δ −1.28 pp), yet remain well above the EA's 2.80%, sustaining above-average cost pressure on Slovak firms.

† Bunky s menej ako 10 platnými odpoveďami v danej kombinácii vlny × krajiny × položky sú vynechané z grafu; medzery v sérii indikujú nedostatok dát pre dané obdobie.

🤖 Táto sekcia obsahuje dáta získané AI agentom priamym dopytovaním databázy SAFE počas generovania správy.

Slovak firms see stronger upside inflation risks than euro area peers

Inflation Risk Outlook (Q33)

  • A net 59% of Slovak firms (n=229) saw upside risks to five-year inflation expectations, rising 5.8 pp from a net 53% in the previous wave — above the euro area's net 55% (n=5,278), implying firms anticipate sustained price pressures locally.
  • Slovakia's upside inflation risk balance has exceeded the euro area's in both the latest and prior wave, suggesting local price pressures are perceived as more persistent than the broader EA average.
  • The euro area's net upside balance rose more sharply — up 9.9 pp to net 55% — than Slovakia's 5.8 pp gain, narrowing but not closing the gap between Slovak and EA inflation risk sentiment, indicating a relative moderation in Slovakia's perceived risk divergence.

† Bunky s menej ako 10 platnými odpoveďami v danej kombinácii vlny × krajiny × položky sú vynechané z grafu; medzery v sérii indikujú nedostatok dát pre dané obdobie.

Slovak firms rate access to finance more pressing while all other concerns ease

Most Pressing Business Problems (Q0B)

  • Slovak firms rated access to finance at 3.91 out of 10 (n=223), rising 0.19 points from the previous quarter's 3.72 — the only business problem in Slovakia to increase in pressingness, against a trend of easing across all other categories, highlighting growing financial strain.
  • By contrast, the EA average access-to-finance score fell 0.15 points to 3.99 (n=5,212), meaning Slovakia's score, while still below the EA's, is narrowing the gap that previously stood at 0.42 points and now stands at just 0.08 points, suggesting Slovak firms are catching up to peers in perceived financing challenges.
  • Costs of production or labour remained the most pressing concern for Slovak firms (score 6.19, n=228), though it eased 0.15 points from the prior quarter and stayed below both the EA (6.48) and DE (6.47) averages, indicating persistent but slightly improving cost pressures.

Zdroj: ECB SAFE mikrodáta. Čistá bilancia = % podnikov hlásiacich nárast mínus % podnikov hlásiacich pokles. Kladná hodnota = sprísnenie / rast (nepriaznivé pre firmy, ak nie je uvedené inak). Záporná hodnota = uvoľnenie / pokles.
